items.title: Key pathophysiology
items.contentHtml:
Failure to relax the LES

  • Failure to relax the LES is due to loss of inhibitory neurons (nitric oxide, VIP) in the myenteric plexus.
  • The pressure of the LES at rest is usually elevated (>45 mmHg) and does not relax with swallowing.
  • High-resolution esophageal manometry (HRM) is the gold standard: diagnostic criterion It is integrated relaxation pressure (IRP) >15 mmHg.
  • The Chicago classification (3.0) defines it as type I (aperistalsis), II (panesogic pressurization) or III (spastic contractions).

items.title: Definition and classification
items.contentHtml:
Primary esophageal motility disorder

  • It is the most studied primary esophageal motility disorder, of idiopathic cause.
  • It is characterized by esophageal aperistalsis and failure to relax the LES.
  • It must be differentiated from secondary achalasia (pseudoachalasia) due to tumor gastric (especially cardia adenocarcinoma), Chagas disease, or malignant infiltration.
  • The typical age of presentation is 30-60 years, with no predominance of sex.

items.foco: Progressive dysphagia for solids and liquids
items.title: Cardinal symptom
items.contentHtml:
Progressive dysphagia for solids and liquids

  • Progressive dysphagia for solids and liquids is the most common symptom (>90% of cases).
  • Unlike peptic stenosis (only solids), in achalasia dysphagia occurs for both from the early stages.
  • Progression is slow (months to years) and may be accompanied by weight loss.
  • At diagnosis Differential should be considered eosinophilic esophagitis (intermittent dysphagia, atopy) and esophageal stricture (solids only).

items.title: Associated symptom
items.contentHtml:
Regurgitation of undigested food

  • Regurgitation of undigested food occurs minutes to hours after eating, without nausea or straining.
  • It can cause nocturnal aspiration (cough, recurrent pneumonia) and halitosis.
  • It is differentiated from vomiting by the absence of retching and gastric contents (acid).
  • Regurgitation is more common in advanced achalasia and is associated with megaesophagus.

items.title: Gold standard diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Study of choice: esophageal manometry

  • The study of choice: esophageal manometry (HRM) confirms the diagnosis with sensitivity >95%.
  • Findings: esophageal aperistalsis (absence of peristaltic waves), elevated LES pressure at rest and failure to relax the LES (elevated IRP).
  • The Chicago classification (v3.0) subdivides into types I, II and III, with prognostic and therapeutic implications.
  • Conventional manometry (non-HRM) is still acceptable, but HRM is preferred.

items.title: Manometric finding
items.contentHtml:
Esophageal aperistalsis in manometry

  • esophageal aperistalsis in manometry is defined as the absence of propagated peristaltic waves in ≥90% of swallows.
  • In achalasia type I there are no contractions; in type II there is panesogic pressurization; in type III there are premature spastic contractions.
  • Aperistalsis differentiates achalasia from other disorders such as diffuse esophageal spasm (simultaneous contractions but preserved peristalsis).

items.title: manometric criterion
items.contentHtml:
Elevated resting LES pressure

  • Elevated resting LES pressure (>45 mmHg) is a classic finding, although not always present.
  • Elevated resting LES pressure is associated with smooth muscle hypertrophy.
  • In high-resolution manometry, IRP (integrated relaxation pressure) >15 mmHg is the key parameter to define failure to relax the LES.
  • Do not confuse with isolated LES hypertension (without aperistalsis), which is not achalasia.

items.title: Classic image
items.contentHtml:
'Bird beak' sign on esophagram

  • The 'bird beak' sign on esophagram (or beak sign) is the sharp distal stricture of the esophagus with proximal dilation.
  • It is best seen with thick barium or barium tablet.
  • This finding is highly suggestive but not pathognomonic (it can be seen in pseudoachalasia).
  • The sensitivity of the esophagram for achalasia is ~70%, lower than manometry.

items.title: Radiological sign
items.contentHtml:
Esophagram with proximal esophageal dilation

  • The esophagram with proximal esophageal dilation shows the dilated esophagus (diameter >4 cm) and can be classified into grades (I: <4 cm, II: 4-6 cm, III: >6 cm).
  • proximal esophageal dilation is associated with retention of food and saliva (level hydroaerial).
  • In advanced stages (megaesophagus) the esophagus can take on an S-shaped shape (sigmoid esophagus).

items.foco: Initial treatment: pneumatic dilation or myotomy
items.title: First line of management
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment: pneumatic dilation or myotomy

  • The initial treatment: pneumatic dilation or myotomy are first-line options, with similar efficacy (~85-90% at 1 year).
  • pneumatic dilation (30-40 mm balloon) is performed under fluoroscopy, with the risk of perforation (~2-5%).
  • Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication (Dor or Toupet) is the standard surgery.
  • Botulinum toxin (injection of 80-100 U) is an alternative in non-candidate patients, with a temporary effect (6-12 months).

items.foco: Heller myotomy as a surgical option
items.title: Definitive surgery
items.contentHtml:
Heller myotomy as a surgical option

  • The Heller myotomy as a surgical option consists of sectioning the muscle fibers of the LES and distal esophagus (6-8 cm).
  • It is always associated with partial fundoplication (Dor anterior or Toupet posterior) to prevent gastroesophageal reflux.
  • The long-term success rate is >90% and recurrence <10%.
  • Complications: esophageal perforation (<1%), postoperative reflux (~10-20%), and persistent dysphagia.

items.foco: Risk of megaesophagus and squamous cell carcinoma
items.title: Late complications
items.contentHtml:
Risk of megaesophagus and squamous carcinoma

  • The risk of megaesophagus and squamous carcinoma increases in long-standing achalasia (>10-15 years).
  • Megaesophagus (diameter >6 cm) is associated with chronic retention, esophagitis and increased risk of aspiration.
  • Carcinoma Squamous cell carcinoma of the esophagus has an incidence 10-50 times higher than in the general population.
  • There is no consensus for routine screening; Some authors recommend endoscopy every 3 years in patients with >10 years of evolution.
